TextENGNew: The body... was gone.
Thanks to that, I hadn't been arrested by the police... but I didn't know whether to be relieved.
...Just as Satoko had said... I had... failed to kill her uncle.
...Since the body wasn't there, no matter what freak of nature or miraculous coincidence I factored in, I was forced to reach that conclusion.
...I had wanted to free Satoko... so I became a demon.
And as my punishment, I had fallen into this abnormal world... yes, metaphorically speaking, a demon's world.
changeReason: Old translates 報い as 'reward' which is the opposite of the JPN meaning (punishment/retribution).

TextENGNew: But that price was supposed to be paid in exchange for the uncle's life.
...Since the uncle was still alive, still shackling Satoko... I would have to become a demon once more.
...One more time.
...No—as many times as it took.@k...I would keep killing that man until Satoko was free.
...I had woken up.
...The clock showed it was just approaching 5 a.m.@kOutside, dawn was already breaking.
When I steeled my resolve to become a demon again...@k...I felt the awakened power surge through my entire body once more.
This wasn't a matter of timing.@k...I would do it.
I sat up.
...The exhaustion of the past two days had been completely purged, and I didn't even feel drowsy.
I rose slowly to my feet and confirmed that my body was responding exactly as I willed it.
I could feel the blood flowing to the tips of every single finger.
...While getting dressed, I let my thoughts wander... and a strange idea came to me.
For these two days... while claiming it was for Satoko's sake, all I'd thought about was killing a man.
While claiming it was for Satoko... during the act itself, I had completely forgotten about her, even if only temporarily.
...Perhaps the trigger had been Satoko's sake.
But the me of right now... was nothing but a demon, killing for the sake of killing.@kAnd as a result, I'd fallen into a world befitting a demon.
...Had it been the same... for Satoshi?
He'd resolved to kill the aunt to save Satoko... became a demon... and vanished from sunlit Hinamizawa, falling into the demon's world... That was why he disappeared.
...In the end, I'd followed the exact same path as Satoshi.
...I'd been determined not to repeat his mistakes, but it turned out the same regardless.
...If that was the case... then Satoshi might be here too.
Having arrived a year before me... Satoshi was somewhere in this world.
At that thought... I froze... and stopped walking.
...That extra set of footsteps I'd grown so accustomed to hearing.
"...........Could you... by any chance... be Satoshi...?"
The empty void, devoid of any presence, offered no reply.
...And yet I felt a strange reassurance... as if someone was there... as if Satoshi had been walking beside me all along.
...Let's go, Satoshi.
...One more time.
...This time, we'll set Satoko free with our own hands.
I slipped out of the house quietly, so my parents wouldn't notice.
If they caught me now, after I'd been staying out so late these past few days, I'd get an earful.@k...I couldn't waste time on that right now.
Today... I would put an end to this.
If the corpse disappeared when I killed him, then I didn't need to bother burying it. I could burn it to ash... or keep the body with me so I could kill him every time he came back.
I would do it again.@k...I would kill him again.@k...This time, I would liberate her.
Outside... the morning light was breathtakingly beautiful...
From the storage shed, I searched for a weapon that would kill him with absolute certainty this time.
...Something blatantly lethal was perfectly fine.
changeReason: Old says 'Blunt or not'; Japanese 露骨な means blatantly obvious/lethal, not blunt. New is correct.

TextENGNew: Whether someone witnessed it, whether the police came—I didn't need to worry about that now.
This world had gone insane, after all.
A world where another me appeared at the festival and played, even when I wasn't there.
changeReason: Old uses first person 'I'd shown up'; Japanese says 勝手に俺が現れて (another me appeared on its own). New correctly uses 'another me.'

TextENGNew: ...Even if the police arrested me, another me would probably just walk through the front door as if nothing had happened.
changeReason: Old says 'I'd probably end up going right back home'; new correctly says 'another me would probably walk through the door,' matching もうひとりの俺.

TextENGNew: ...That other me was also 'Keiichi Maebara.'
changeReason: Old includes self ('Both me and...'); Japanese refers only to the double. New is more accurate.

TextENGNew: Even without me, he would take my place.
...I could vanish from Hinamizawa and no one would notice.
...So in other words... it didn't matter if I went down with him.
changeReason: Old says 'mistakenly killed'; Japanese 刺し違えてもいい means mutual destruction/going down together. New is correct.

TextENGNew: Even if I died, 'Keiichi Maebara' would remain.
...After all... this wasn't the world I belonged in.@k...If I could sacrifice myself for Satoko's sake, that too was a Nii-nii's duty.
changeReason: Old says 'killed mistakenly'; Japanese 刺し違えられるなら means 'if I could sacrifice myself.' New correctly translates.

TextENGNew: ...When I embraced that reckless abandon... the morning air somehow felt wonderfully fresh.
"...This one's... terrifying.@k@vS03/01/140100916. ...I'll take it."
It was... a hatchet meant for splitting thick firewood.
...Its thick steel blade was sinister, almost as if killing a person were its true intended purpose.
Carrying it out in the open would be ill-advised, so I wrapped it loosely in old newspaper and tossed it in my bicycle's front basket.
"...Sorry for dragging you into dirty business day after day.@k@vS03/01/140100918. ...I'm counting on you again today, old friend."
changeReason: Old says 'my legs'; Japanese 俺の足 here refers to the bicycle. New correctly addresses the bicycle as 'old friend.'

TextENGNew: For some reason, I found myself apologizing to my bicycle.
...These past three days, I'd used it for nothing but dangerous errands.
I'd been riding this bike ever since we lived in the old town.
I could walk most places I wanted to go, so I hadn't used it all that much.
Mom had bought it for me when I started cram school, since the station was far away and she thought it would be convenient.@k...So this bike was actually a gift from her.
changeReason: Old's second segment says 'I'd always used it to get back and forth from cram school'; new says 'this bike was actually a gift from her' -- both partially inaccurate to Japanese, but the meaning shifts differ.

TextENGNew: The front basket had only ever carried textbooks and study materials.
...Under no circumstances had it ever held a hatchet meant for killing someone.
...Since coming to Hinamizawa, I'd finally begun to feel that life was worth living.
changeReason: Old says 'life finally felt fun'; Japanese 生きていて楽しい means 'being alive was enjoyable.' New's 'life was worth living' captures deeper meaning.

TextENGNew: I'd met the greatest friends imaginable and had the most wonderful times.
...Those times... might have been shattered now, but...
...I wished with all my heart that I could get them back, even if it meant risking my life.
...That was how precious those times had been.
I had killed a person.
...And now here I was, heading out to kill again.
Murder was a sin.
changeReason: Japanese 罪 in this moral/introspective context means 'sin' rather than 'crime'; new translation reflects this.

TextENGNew: ...There should probably never be a murder that could be justified.
But... even so.@k...The joyful times I'd been given were more than enough to justify committing that sin.
Irreplaceable friends. Irreplaceable moments together.
...Goofing around, laughing, sometimes deceiving each other, yet always the best of friends.
To reclaim those joyful, soul-enriching times, I feared no sin.
Yes.@kThis was a value I had chosen for myself.
...It wasn't something any teacher had taught me in a classroom.
...It was a noble path... that I had chosen of my own volition.
Memories of my days since coming to Hinamizawa flooded back.
changeReason: Old says 'thought back to when I'd first come'; Japanese 日々が蘇る means all the days came flooding back, not just arrival.

TextENGNew: On my very first day, Satoko had welcomed me with a blackboard eraser loaded with a rock.
...That 'welcome' had truly knocked the wind out of me.
She laughed, got angry, cried—she was never boring for a moment.
...She was clearly the most childish of all of us, yet she was surprisingly resourceful and loved sticking her nose into everyone's business—a lively handful.
The same went for Mion, Rena, and Rika-chan.@kWasn't Satoko always at the center, with everyone laughing and teasing around her?
Satoko would put on airs.
Then Mion would tease her mercilessly until she was cornered.
Then she'd cry, and Rena would go into raptures.
Rika-chan would look on with profound satisfaction.
...And I'd get caught in the crossfire.
...We were always carrying on like that.
...It was lonely when anyone was missing, but if I had to name one person... it would be Satoko.
...Her smile was everyone's smile.
When she stopped smiling, the smiles disappeared from all of us.
And for us, losing our smiles... was the same as being dead.
...It was no different from those days of silently trudging to cram school, maintaining a stagnant test score.
In short... without her smile, we were nothing.
She was full of bluster, but she was our adorable, lovable princess.
...Then I, setting off for the dragon's castle to reclaim her smile, must be something like her knight.

``...Knights, of course, use swords.@k@vS03/01/140100920. The awesome two-handed kind!@k@vS03/01/140100921. ...I'm probably the only knight in the history of the world... to be taking someone on with a thick, rusty hatchet.``
changeReason: Reverted: truncated: loses the self-deprecating joke about being the only knight with a rusty hatchet

TextENGNew: Just then, an old man waved at me from the ridge between rice paddies.
changeReason: Old says 'old person in the ridge of a field'; new correctly specifies 'old man' and 'ridge between rice paddies.'

TextENGNew: "...Well, well!@k@vS03/00/old04002. Keiichi-kun!@k@vS03/00/old04003. You're up early!"
changeReason: Old translates 朝のサイクリングかぃね (morning cycling?) as 'Out for a morning ride'; new says 'You're up early!' losing the cycling reference.

TextENGNew: "Good mooorning!!"
I reflexively waved back with a bright smile.
...Should I really have been... waving so cheerfully...?
Past these rice paddies, Satoko's house was just ahead.
The cicadas had woken up too... and before I knew it, the usual daytime chorus was in full swing.
...I smacked my head a couple of times... driving out my carefree morning mood.
I exhaled deeply... tilted my head back... and quieted the useless excitement...
...The body... hadn't been there.
Its absence meant either that my killing him had been an illusion... or that he had come back to life. One or the other.
...If I'd been at the festival... then perhaps the illusion explanation was more reasonable.
...Coach had probably thought the same, which was why he'd treated me as if I were deranged.
...But it being an illusion... that alone was impossible.
I had killed him. Without a doubt.@k...Without a doubt.
If that had been an illusion... then this entire Hinamizawa I was standing in now would be an illusion too.
...If that were the case, here's how the story would end:
...I was actually in a coma from a traffic accident, lying in a hospital bed, dreaming of living in the countryside with wonderful friends like these...@k ...A terrifying thought, though.
...If it wasn't an illusion... then the only remaining possibility was that he'd come back to life.@k...That would make him, quite literally, a monster.
But... if he was a monster that could resurrect... then I was a monster too.
...I didn't hesitate to kill, and what's more... I could even invoke curses.
Takano-san's death was surely the result of my curse.
...Maybe that was a stretch.
...If Coach and Ooishi died too—the ones I'd cursed yesterday—then I'd believe it.
I set my bicycle aside... and went to survey the area.
His motorcycle wasn't here.
...Of course it wasn't.@k...I'd hurled it into the Onigafuchi swamp that night, sent it down to the realm of demons.
changeReason: Old says 'sent it down to Hell'; Japanese says 鬼の国 (realm of demons/oni), not Hell. New correctly says 'realm of demons.'

TextENGNew: ...Or maybe...@kjust as the corpse had vanished... the motorcycle, too, had never been thrown into the swamp...?
The motorcycle being absent might not be because I'd dumped it in the swamp—maybe he'd simply ridden it into town and hadn't come back yet.
...If the uncle wasn't here, that was convenient in its own way.
...In that case, I'd just let myself in, make myself comfortable, and sharpen my hatchet while I waited for him to return.
...But if he was here...
...........From the pit of my stomach, a sensation I'd nearly forgotten.
...Tension, rising.
...This feeling was a poison that slowed my actions... but it was also the detonator for a superhuman awakening that transcended the self.
I gripped the hatchet, still wrapped in newspaper.
...Its solid heft was both sinister and reassuring.
...I hadn't worn my watch, so I didn't know the exact time... but it was probably still before 7 a.m.
Satoko would likely be awake, preparing breakfast and lunches.
...But the uncle was surely still wallowing in bed.
A man like him probably drank heavily at night, stayed up late, and slept until noon.
...I considered ringing the doorbell and having Satoko let me in, but the sound might wake the uncle, and I'd lose the advantage of catching him asleep.
If Satoko saw me slipping inside without ringing... she'd surely be startled.
...If I ran into Satoko... what would I say?
'I've come to kill your uncle for you, so stay quiet.'
...No, that wouldn't work.
...That would be like making Satoko complicit in his murder.
...'Be quiet and go wait outside.'
...Maybe that would do.
How would... Satoko react?
...She would probably... try to stop me from killing him.
...I'd need some kind of lie to get her out of the house.
...Satoko didn't need to know that a murder was going to happen in her home.
...I couldn't think of a suitable lie just yet... but I couldn't stand here forever.
I steeled myself... and put my hand on the doorknob...
Slowly... I turned it.@k...Then... pulled.
...It wasn't locked.
...The gap in the door widened steadily.@k...There was no chain, either.
The oppressive smell of daily life in Satoko's house washed over me.
...At the entrance... several pairs of shoes and sandals lay scattered about.
...Just looking at the shoes, I couldn't tell whether anyone was home.
changeReason: Old assumes 'he' (the uncle); Japanese 不在かどうか doesn't specify who. New's 'whether anyone was home' is more faithful.

TextENGNew: ...I could hear a television playing somewhere.
The television... was in the messy dining room.
...Food was scattered across the table... painting a picture of mealtimes utterly devoid of warmth.
...Meals were supposed to be eaten with gratitude toward the person who prepared them.
...If that was the standard, then whatever had taken place in this dining room was no meal at all.
No one in sight.
...No sign of anyone.@k...But with the TV still on... someone had to be nearby.
changeReason: Old specifies 'he must have been close'; Japanese just says someone must be nearby. New correctly says 'someone.'

TextENGNew: ...Could he have anticipated my arrival and be hiding, holding his breath, waiting to ambush me...?
...The tension was so taut, so knife-edge tight, it hurt.
The thick blade of the hatchet lost none of its power wrapped in newspaper.
...I tightened my grip on it and cautiously surveyed the area...
Where had that peaceful feeling from my ride here gone?
...Even the faint sizzle of sweat beading on my forehead... felt deafeningly loud...
That's when... I looked at the table and noticed something.
...This wasn't breakfast.@kThe grains of rice were dried to a hard crust.
...Then... was this last night's dinner...?
Still on guard, I left the dining room... followed the hallway and found the staircase to the second floor.
...That man had poked his face out of a second-floor window when he'd been socializing with his friends.
...That was most likely his bedroom, and there was a high chance he was sleeping there.
I placed my toes on the edges of the stairs... and crept up... so quietly... so carefully...
...I couldn't hear any snoring.
...No sign of anyone.
I checked each room, gently sliding open fusuma doors, peering inside...@k...but found no trace of anyone.
To search more thoroughly... I pressed myself flat against the floor and put my ear to it.
...All I could hear was... the television downstairs... and some low humming sound, like a water heater.
...It was around this point... that a creeping sense of wrongness began to take root in me.
changeReason: Both 'feelings of suspicion' and 'creeping sense of wrongness' are valid translations of 不審感; the difference is stylistic rather than substantial.

TextENGNew: This house... something was off.
There were signs of life—the TV was on—but... for the house to be empty this early in the morning was strange.
I descended the stairs without a sound... and re-entered the dining room.
I looked at the food scattered about.
...Bone-dry grains of rice.
Miso soup splashed across the surface.
changeReason: Old says 'An overturned bowl of miso soup'; Japanese ぶちまけられた means splashed/scattered. New is more accurate.

TextENGNew: A wrapper from side dishes bought at the supermarket.
...There was no doubt: Satoko had prepared this.
...It lacked both Mion's polish and Rena's gentle touch, but... it was a meal made with genuine care.
...Judging from the meals she'd cooked at my house... and the types and quantities of side dishes... I estimated this was last night's dinner.
The numbers stamped on the side dish package read 830620.
...June 20, 1983.@k...That was yesterday.
And the food was set for two.
...In other words... this was last night's dinner, and at that time, both Satoko and the uncle had been here.
changeReason: Old mentions only uncle; Japanese explicitly says both Satoko and the uncle were here. New includes both.

TextENGNew: ...Stop it, Keiichi.
...Stop saying or thinking the word 'impossible'...
Regardless... the uncle had been here last night.@k...That much was fact.
He'd found fault with Satoko's cooking... hurled the miso soup bowl... and scattered the meal everywhere.
...Deep beneath my supposedly calm composure... a whispered 'damn him' and a spark of fury ignited.
changeReason: Old says 'a curse' (could be misread as supernatural); Japanese 畜生 means 'damn him.' New correctly translates.

TextENGNew: ...Did that man... deserve even a shred of mercy...?
...Grrunk.@k...Mmmmmmmmm...
That humming sound, the one I'd been hearing all along, caught my attention again.@k...What was it?
...The first floor...?@k...But where?
...The bathroom... maybe...?
The sound was coming from behind the frosted glass door of the changing room.
...It had to be the bath's water heater.
...My suspicions deepened further.
...People didn't normally draw a bath this early in the morning.
If one was running...
...then in this house... time had stopped at some point last night.
...And then... a jolt of electricity shot through my mind... and a terrible chill gripped me.

changeReason: Old says 'Could she...?!' specifying female; Japanese まさか is gender-neutral disbelief. New is more accurate.

TextENGNew: I swallowed hard... then slid the changing room door open.
changeReason: Old says 'pulled aside the glass door'; Japanese says 脱衣室の戸を横に引く (slid the changing room door). New is more accurate.

TextENGNew: "...Sa-... Satokoooooo!!"
Satoko had collapsed on the floor of the changing room, unconscious.
I immediately scooped up her small body and laid her on a bath towel. ...She was burning with fever.
Why were you drawing a bath in this condition?! Of course it would only make things worse!
changeReason: Old says 'taking a bath'; Japanese なんで風呂なんかに is about drawing/running a bath, not being in it.

TextENGNew: "Hang in there, Satoko!!@k@vS03/01/140100926. It's me—Keiichi!@k@vS03/01/140100927. Satoko! Can you hear me?!"
changeReason: Old says 'It'll be all right' and 'Do you understand?!'; Japanese 大丈夫か means 'Are you all right?' New is more faithful.

TextENGNew: "...Are you all right, Satoko...?@k@vS03/01/140100930. It's going to be okay now.@k@vS03/01/140100931. ...Nii-nii is here..."
changeReason: Old says 'What happened, Satoko?'; Japanese 大丈夫か means 'Are you all right?' New correctly translates.

TextENGNew: Satoko cracked open her vacant eyes and looked up at me, managing an awkward smile.
What the hell was her uncle doing, leaving Satoko like this?!
...That painful, pitiable expression... when I saw it, my emotions snapped and erupted.
"GRAAAAAAHHHHH!!! I'LL KILL YOOOUUUUU!!! RAAAAAAHHHHH!!!"
Raising the newspaper-wrapped hatchet, I stormed through every room.
...I searched for any trace of him—presence, scent, vibration, heartbeat—anything.
With a battle cry, I charged up the stairs with enough force to crack them.
...I found the futon in what I believed was the uncle's room and—knowing full well no one was sleeping there—buried the hatchet in it over and over.
Did he crawl into the closet?!
I smashed through the closet doors with the hatchet!!
Not here either... is there a hidden door?!@kIs there some mechanism in these walls?!
I battered wall after wall with the hatchet, smashing them apart.
...Dust billowed up, and shattered debris littered the floor.
I smashed every glass window too, for good measure.
"WOOOOOHHHHHHHHHHH!!! ...Hah... hah... hah...!@k@vS03/01/140100937. Where are you... you piece of...!!"
Once I had devastated the uncle's room beyond all recognition... my reason finally wrested control from my emotions...
The fact was... the uncle was not in this house right now.
...Killing the uncle would have to wait...@kRight now... getting Satoko help came first...!!
I returned to the changing room.
...Satoko was still lying limp, probably because of her fever.
...There was nothing more an amateur like me could do.@k...I needed to take her... to the clinic.
...Coach... had treated me as a lunatic yesterday.
changeReason: Old adds 'and tried to lock me up in a mental hospital' not present in this Japanese line.

TextENGNew: ...I never wanted to see that man's face again... but even so... I had to go to the clinic one more time...
...I had to get a doctor to look at Satoko...
In the laundry basket, I found Satoko's familiar school uniform, crumpled inside.@kIt was grimy... far too dirty to put on her now.
changeReason: Old rearranges content from multiple Japanese segments; new follows the Japanese more faithfully for this specific line.

TextENGNew: ...Damn it... I didn't have time to waste fumbling around with this...!
changeReason: Old says 'eventually gave up on clothing her and decided to carry her in her bath towel' -- this is the content of the NEXT line. Japanese for this line is the cursing about fumbling. Misaligned translation.

TextENGNew: In the end, I gave up on dressing her and decided to carry Satoko on my back, wrapped in a bath towel.
changeReason: Old says 'The clinic was close to Satoko's house' -- this is from a different line entirely. Japanese says he gave up dressing her and decided to carry her wrapped in a towel.

TextENGNew: It would be far faster to carry her to the nearby clinic than to call 119 for an ambulance.
changeReason: Old loses 'nearby clinic'; new includes it. Japanese says 背負っていった方がはるかに早い (carrying her would be far faster).

TextENGNew: I draped the largest bath towel I could find over her and urged her to stand.
"...Can you stand...?@k@vS03/01/140100945. I'll carry you to the clinic on my back."
Her words were clearer than before.
Knowing she was even slightly improving, tears of relief spilled down my cheeks.
changeReason: Old says 'She was starting to come back'; Japanese says 安堵の涙がこぼれた (tears of relief spilled). Completely different content.

TextENGNew: Satoko rolled over onto her stomach, and... struggled weakly to get her knees under her.
changeReason: Old contains the tears of relief content that belongs to the previous line; Japanese for this line describes Satoko rolling onto her stomach and struggling to stand.

TextENGNew: "We'll be there soon... just hang on a little longer...!"
Carrying Satoko's impossibly light body caused me no strain at all.@k...But that very lightness... filled me with dread.
Outside, the heat had intensified, as though conspiring to torment Satoko further...
Damn cicadas... Why did it have to be this scorching on this particular morning...
I didn't have time to curse them.@k...The clinic... was this way.
With Satoko on my back, I broke into a run...
